data.title: Bezos' Blue Origin pauses New Shepard rocket program to focus on moon lander efforts
data.description:
By Joey Roulette

Jeff Bezos' space company Blue Origin on Friday said it was "pausing" flights of New Shepard, the company's centerpiece space tourism rocket, to shift resources to its moon lander program with NASA.

The company said "it will pause its New Shepard flights and shift resources to further accelerate development of the company's human lunar capabilities."

New Shepard is a 60-foot tall reusable rocket that has launched dozens of paying passengers and research experiments from Texas on short suborbital flights since 2021.

data.title: These Stocks Are Today's Movers: Apple, Verizon, SoFi, Sandisk, Western Digital, Unity Software, Exxon, and More — Barrons.com
data.description:
By Mackenzie Tatananni, Callum Keown, and Joe Woelfel

Stocks were falling Friday after President Donald Trump selected Kevin Warsh as his nominee for the next chair of the Federal Reserve and data showed U.S. producer price inflation for December was higher than expected.

These stocks were making moves:

Apple fell 1.5%. The iPhone maker posted fiscal first-quarter earnings that beat Wall Street estimates as revenue jumped 16% to $143.8 billion. Revenue for the iPhone, particularly, jumped 23% and topped forecasts.The stock slipped, however, on concerns over rising costs, particularly for memory components.

Sandisk jumped 11%. The memory supplier reported fiscal second-quarter adjusted profit that smashed analysts' estimates. The company, which spun off from Western Digital last year, said it expects third-quarter adjusted earnings in the range of $12 to $14 a share, well above Wall Street forecasts of $5.11. "The critical role that our products play in powering AI and the world's technology is being recognized," said CEO David Goeckeler.

Western Digital, meanwhile, fell 9.3%. The data-storage company posted fiscal second-quarter adjusted earnings that beat expectations. Revenue in the period jumped 25% to $3.02 billion on rising demand for hard-disk drives. Western Digital said it expects third-quarter revenue of $3.2 billion at the midpoint of its range. Analysts has been expecting revenue of $2.98 billion.

KLA Corp., which makes equipment for the semiconductor industry, sank 13%. Although second-quarter earnings and revenue beat analysts' forecasts, shares had run up into the print, rising 39% this year and 127% over the past 12 months. The Nasdaq Composite gained 1.9% and 20%, respectively, over the same periods.

Verizon Communications jumped 9.7%, putting the stock on pace for its largest same-day percentage increase in more than two years. The wireless carrier posted better-than-anticipated adjusted earnings and revenue for the fourth quarter. Verizon added 616,000 postpaid phone subscribers in the period, above the 417,000 analysts had expected and the 504,000 subscribers it added last year..

SoFi Technologies pared back earlier gains and fell 5.3%. The fintech reported a strong fourth quarter, with adjusted net revenue reaching $1 billion for the first time, and revenue growing across all three of its core business segments. CEO Anthony Noto described the results as "exceptional" in an interview with Barron's.

American Express slid 3.7% on mixed quarterly earnings. The company's expenses increased 10% to $14.5 billion in the fourth quarter, largely driven by higher variable customer engagement costs from increased customer spending and the release of its new Platinum cards.

Microsoft fell 0.7%. Shares of the software giant slumped 10% Thursday on concerns over slowing growth for the company's Azure cloud computing platform and heavy capital expenditures. The stock's decline was its largest single-day percentage drop since March 16, 2020, when it fell nearly 15%, according to Dow Jones Market Data. Microsoft lost $357 billion in market cap Thursday, its largest one-day decline on record.

Meta Platforms declined 2.9%. The stock closed up 10% on Thursday as the parent company of Facebook and Instagram issued a forecast that called for better-than-expected first-quarter revenue guidance and said it expects to significantly increase spending on artificial intelligence. The stock has risen almost 12% in January, putting it on pace for its best month since June 2025, according to Dow Jones Market Data.

Chevron advanced 1.7% after the oil company beat consensus views for the fourth quarter. Adjusted earnings of $1.52 a share topped analysts' calls for $1.42. Revenue of $46.87 billion beat expectations for $46.79 billion, but fell 10% from the same period last year.

Exxon Mobil fell 1%. The oil major posted earnings and revenue that surpassed Wall Street's expectations, but profit for its production business declined amid falling crude prices.

Visa fell 2.8%. The payments giant reported fiscal first-quarter earnings and revenue that beat Wall Street estimates, boosted by strong card user spending. Visa and peer Mastercard have been under pressure following President Donald Trump's call for a one-year, 10% cap on credit card interest rates.

Deckers Outdoor surged 15% after the owner of the Ugg and Hoka shoe brands posted fiscal third-quarter earnings that topped analysts' forecasts and issued better-than-expected fiscal 2026 guidance. Hoka net sales in the third quarter jumped 19% to $628.9 million.

Take-Two Interactive Software fell 9.2%, Roblox slumped 11%, and Unity Software cratered 21% after Alphabet-owned Google unveiled Project Genie, a so-called AI world model that lets users create and explore digital spaces. The prototype is currently available to Google AI Ultra subscribers in the U.S., the company said.

Write to Mackenzie Tatananni at mackenzie.tatananni@barrons.com and Callum Keown at callum.keown@dowjones.com.

This content was created by Barron's, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. Barron's is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal.

data.title: Cincinnati Financial Raises Dividend by 8%
data.description:
By Katherine Hamilton

Cincinnati Financial raised its quarterly cash dividend by 8% to 94 cents a share from 87 cents.

The new payout, equal to $3.76 a year, represents an annual yield of 2.4% based on Thursday's closing price of $158.19.

The insurance provider said Friday it would pay the new dividend on April 15 to shareholders of record as of March 24.

Write to Katherine Hamilton at katherine.hamilton@wsj.com

data.title: Tevogen Bio Holdings Inc Evaluates Special Cash Dividend
data.description:
On January 30, 2026, Tevogen Bio Holdings Inc announced its Board's intention to evaluate a special cash dividend for shareholders.

Key Highlights:

  • Dividend contingent on future financial milestones.
  • Evaluation includes revenue and earnings targets.
  • Subject to financial performance and capital needs.
  • Final approval required from Board of Directors.
  • No assurance on timing or amount of dividend.

data.title: Livestock Futures Fall as U.S. Cattle Herd Changes Little — Market Talk
data.description: Livestock futures slip as lean hogs give back some of yesterday's gains triggered by strong pork export sales. Live cattle fall, but remain near all-time highs. The USDA's Cattle Inventory report, after markets close, gives no indication that beef supply is about to ease. As of January 1, the U.S. cattle herd numbered 86.2 million head, slightly below the 86.5 million head a year earlier. Beef cows, at 27.6 million head, were down 1% from a year ago, while beef replacement heifers were up 1%. On the CBOT, the most-active live cattle contract falls 0.2% to $2.368 a pound. Lean hogs drop 0.3%, to 95.15 cents a pound. (paulo.trevisani@wsj.com; @ptrevisani)

data.title: Bond market isn't getting everything it wants from Trump's choice of Warsh for Fed chair
data.description:
By Vivien Lou Chen

'Nominating Warsh avoids a lot of the worst-case scenarios, but it's still unclear what impact he will have on rates,' strategist says

President Donald Trump has selected former Federal Reserve governor Kevin Warsh as his nominee to be the next head of the central bank.

Traders in the roughly $30 trillion Treasury market were assessing President Donald Trump's nominee to be the next leader of the Federal Reserve and coming to the conclusion that they aren't going to end up getting everything they want.

On Friday, the bond market was trading in a manner that suggested there were some worries over Trump's selection of former Fed governor Kevin Warsh to replace Jerome Powell, whose term as Fed chair ends in May. Long-dated Treasury yields edged slightly higher, while shorter-term rates dipped - producing a wider gap between the two, known as a steeper curve. In particular, the concern is that Warsh, if approved by Congress, might push for more interest-rate cuts than are currently expected, which could end up creating inflation problems in the long run.

"Nominating Warsh avoids a lot of the worst-case scenarios, but it's still unclear what impact he will have on rates," said strategist Will Compernolle at FHN Financial in Chicago. For now, the bond market is telegraphing that "the Fed's current interest-rate trajectory under Fed Chair Jerome Powell is the correct way to manage risks." Fed officials have penciled in just one quarter-point rate cut for 2026, while fed-funds futures traders mostly expect two reductions by year-end.

If Warsh sticks closer to Trump's preferred stance on interest rates, he might push for more cuts than expected and a decrease in the size of the Fed's balance sheet, which is why 10-year BX:TMUBMUSD10Y and 30-year Treasury yields BX:TMUBMUSD30Y inched up by a few basis points each to almost 4.25% and 4.88%, respectively, on Friday, according to the strategist. The rate on the long bond intermittently surpassed 4.9% and was heading for its highest level in more than a week.

Read: Here's what Trump's pick of Kevin Warsh to run the Fed means for the economy, markets and you

Meanwhile, the 5-year breakeven inflation rate, a gauge of average expected inflation over the next five years, hovered just above 2.5% on Friday, reflecting growing worries about sustained upside risks to inflation. The rate was as low as 2.28% at the start of the year, according to FactSet. Market participants had been so worried about Trump selecting someone who was going to "trash the institution" that the selection of Warsh is seen as "still within the margin of reasonableness," Compernolle said in a phone interview. The concern now is that Warsh might favor easier monetary policy at the expense of longer-term inflation, Compernolle added.

Warsh is a former Fed governor who quit the central bank in 2011 shortly after it embarked on a second round of bond purchases under then-Chair Ben Bernanke. His departure earned him a reputation as someone who was a bit more aggressive than his colleagues about limiting the Fed's footprint in markets through the use of its balance sheet. He has more recently advocated in favor of lower rates and a smaller Fed balance sheet. Warsh told Fox News last year that Trump is right to be frustrated with the Fed's policies under Powell.

"Warsh's views are going to evolve over time," said Derek Tang, an economist at Monetary Policy Analytics, a research and advisory firm co-founded by former Fed governor Larry Meyer. His views from the past, Tang added, are "not a good guide of what he could do as Fed chair."The Fed chair is just one person so Warsh "has a big job on his hands in getting everyone to see his views," which might conflict with what the rest of the rate-setting Federal Open Market Committee wants, Tang said in a phone interview. Still, "it's quite possible he might get one or two rate more cuts through than what's expected, so we could see four cuts in total by the end of year depending on how things go."

-Vivien Lou Chen

This content was created by MarketWatch, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. MarketWatch is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal.

data.title: 10-Year Treasury Yield Rises to 4.240% This Month — Data Talk
data.description:
The 10-year yield rose 0.088 percentage point to 4.240% this month, the price closing at 98 3/32.

  • Yield is up for two consecutive months
  • Yield is up 0.222 percentage point over the last two months
  • Largest two-month yield gain since Jan. 2025
  • This week it is up 0.004 percentage point
  • Yield is up for three consecutive weeks
  • Yield is up 0.070 percentage point over the last three weeks
  • Largest three-week yield gain since the week ending Jan. 16, 2026
  • Yield is up four of the past five weeks
  • Today it is up 0.014 percentage point and the price fell 4/32 price points
  • Yield is up three of the past four trading days
  • Today's yield is the fifth highest this year
  • Yield is off 0.396 percentage point from its 52-week high of 4.636% hit Wednesday, Feb. 12, 2025
  • Yield is up 0.288 percentage point from its 52-week low of 3.952% hit Wednesday, Oct. 22, 2025
  • Yield is down 0.325 percentage point from 52 weeks ago
  • Yield is off 0.053 percentage point from its 2026 closing high of 4.294

% hit Tuesday, Jan. 20, 2026

  • Yield is up 0.104 percentage point from its 2026 closing low of 4.137% hit Wednesday, Jan. 7, 2026
  • Year-to-date the yield is up 0.088 percentage point

Data based on 3 p.m. ET values

Source: Tradeweb FTSE U.S. Treasury Closing Prices

data.title: Why a Ruling Over CK Hutchison's Panama Canal Ports Matters — and What's Next — WSJ
data.description:
By Santiago Pérez, Costas Paris and James T. Areddy

Panama's Supreme Court decision to void Hong Kong-based CK Hutchison's contract to operate two ports at either end of the Panama Canal is rippling through global shipping and geopolitics, complicating a $23 billion ports deal and sharpening U.S.-China competition in the Western Hemisphere.

The ruling forces Hutchison out of the strategically located terminals and deals a setback to Beijing's regional influence. It also hands Trump a symbolic win as his administration presses allies and partners to curb China's footprint in critical infrastructure.

The high court said the license terms granted to the Hong Kong firm violate Panama's constitution. The termination of Hutchison's license to operate the ports of Balboa on the Pacific Coast and Cristóbal on the Atlantic side throws a wrench into the company's plans to sell more than 40 ports globally to BlackRock and Mediterranean Shipping Co. The two Panama container terminals were the crown jewels of the deal.

What the ruling means for the Panama Canal

The ports managed by Hutchison aren't part of the Panama Canal, and the court's decision has no impact on the operation of the waterway.

The decades-old treaty that ceded U.S. control of the canal to Panama bans the involvement of foreign governments or state-run firms. At least 5% of global trade flows through the waterway, which is required to allow passage to vessels from all countries. The U.S. and China are top users.

The container terminals are located at the entrances of the waterway, a proximity that has long magnified their strategic sensitivity despite their formal separation from canal operations. Panama's Port Authority, which supervises the terminals, said on Friday that it has implemented a transition plan to ensure the continuity of operations at the two ports. Once Hutchison's license is formally revoked, it will hire APM Terminals, a unit of shipping firm A.P. Moller-Maersk, to temporarily manage the facilities.

The government will then open a bidding process with new license terms for the facilities, possibly separating the two ports to maximize value and market competition. BlackRock, MSC, APM Terminals and French rival CMA CGM have expressed interest, according to people familiar with the process. Last year Maersk bought the Panama Canal Railway Co., which operates a 47-mile line parallel to the waterway and moves containers between the Pacific and Atlantic coasts, further entrenching its logistics presence around the canal corridor.

Hutchison can't appeal a Supreme Court ruling, but it can request clarifications that could delay the formal termination of its operating license. Hutchison said the decision undermined Panama's business environment. A spokesman for China's Foreign Ministry vowed to defend the country's interests.

The court ruling alters a $23 billion global ports deal

The BlackRock and MSC-led deal to buy Hutchison's global ports portfolio already looked to be in peril, with China's government threatening to block the transaction if state-run shipping company Cosco wasn't added as an equal partner with veto power.

The removal of the Panama container terminals from the transaction, however, could reduce Beijing's leverage over the broader deal, potentially clearing a path to a sale of the remaining ports, which are less politically charged.

The billionaires behind MSC have already discussed how to move forward with the bulk of the deal while the Panama case is resolved, the Journal reported last year. Hutchison's global ports would also be a prized addition for BlackRock's recently acquired infrastructure investing business.

Under the original deal, MSC was set to become the controlling owner of all the ports except for the two in Panama where BlackRock would have a controlling stake, the Journal reported. If the Panama assets are excluded, the parties will need to draw up new terms with Hutchison. A senior Chinese government official said Panama's court ruling complicates the deal but doesn't derail it.

A diplomatic setback for China

The decision adds to pressure on China in the Western Hemisphere and suggests to other governments that they might re-examine their ties to the world's second-biggest economy while Trump is in office.

China in recent years has emerged as the largest trading partner for most nations in Latin America and a source of funding for much-needed infrastructure, including ports and railways, investments that are now facing heightened political and security scrutiny. In Venezuela, for instance, Chinese oil producers gained ground by aligning politically with the regimes of Hugo Chávez and Nicolás Maduro, but now have an uncertain future as the Trump administration asserts control over the sector after Maduro's capture by the U.S.

The Panama ports appeared to provide Beijing little political or financial benefit. Unlike Chinese government-owned companies, Hutchison has long been run like a Western business where profits are paramount. Its ports operation in Panama was formerly led by a Briton. Hutchison's controlling shareholder, 97-year-old Li Ka-shing, has sometimes had a testy relationship with Beijing, in particular since Xi Jinping became China's leader in 2012.

Yet the ouster of the Hong Kong port operator illustrates how American pressure on a small nation can dislodge operations linked, fairly or not, to perceptions of Chinese state influence. The court decision follows Panama's hasty exit last year from China's Belt and Road initiative, Xi's signature international expansion effort that has featured thousands of projects.

"China still has a strong incentive to treat this as a strategic test case and show it will defend Chinese-linked interests when they become targets," said Craig Singleton, senior director of the China Program at Washington think tank Foundation for Defense of Democracies. Singleton said the issue now becomes less about Hutchison and more about "stop the dominoes."

A Trump pledge

Trump said during his inauguration last year that he wanted to take back control of the canal, which the U.S. built in the early 20th century and handed over to Panama in late 1999.

While Panama strongly defended its control over the waterway, Trump's threats accelerated a simmering dispute between Hutchison and the administration of Panama's pro-American President José Raúl Mulino over contract terms.

Panama officials say that canal operations weren't affected by China's economic expansion, and its presence didn't pose a security risk. But there was a growing perception that China was rapidly gaining a foothold and influence in the Central American nation.

"This is a win for America, Panama, and all of our allies who recognize the Canal's importance to national security and the world economy," said Michigan Republican John Moolenaar, chairman of a House Select Committee on China.

Write to Santiago Pérez at santiago.perez@wsj.com, Costas Paris at costas.paris@wsj.com and James T. Areddy at James.Areddy@wsj.com

data.title: Chevron Signals More Venezuelan Heavy Crude in U.S. Refineries — OPIS
data.description:
Chevron could increase Venezuelan heavy crude flows in its U.S. refining system, Chief Executive Officer Mike Wirth said on the company's fourth-quarter 2025 earnings call Friday.

The company currently processes about 50,000 b/d at its Pascagoula, Miss., refinery and could run an additional 100,000 b/d across its Gulf Coast refineries and on the West Coast at El Segundo in California, he added.

Chevron has increased Venezuelan crude production in its joint ventures by about 200,000 b/d since 2022, including deliveries into U.S. refineries. The refiner said production could grow by up to 50% over the next 18-24 months.

The company's downstream operations are well-positioned as California refineries close, Wirth said, citing the state's geographic and logistical isolation and fuel specification constraints, which contribute to higher local fuel prices and stronger refining margins. Chevron also reported its highest U.S. refinery throughput in two decades, reflecting recent expansions and improved operational efficiency.

This content was created by Oil Price Information Service, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. OPIS is run independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal.

Reporting by Allegra Fradkin, afradkin@opisnet.com; Editing by Michael Kelly, mkelly@opisnet.com
